meta.title: Standing facts
meta.note: The durable structural conditions of Mexico — not events, not live numbers, but the facts that frame them. This is the old one-liner 'bottom line' decomposed and sourced. The homepage brief's 'where things stand' paragraph may draw ONLY from these, the board numbers, and the event log — every sentence links to one. Hand-curated; review quarterly.

facts.fact: About 80% of Mexico's exports go to the United States — the deepest single-partner dependence of any large economy.
The USMCA is now in an annual-review cycle running to 2036 after the U.S. declined to renew it in 2026 — the single biggest swing factor for the economy.
Trend growth is weak — external forecasters put 2026 GDP growth near 1%.
A little over half of Mexican workers are in the informal economy, off the formal payroll and tax rolls.
Banxico targets 3% inflation, give or take one point, and sets the policy rate to hold it there.
Remittances from workers in the U.S. run about 3.5% of GDP — a bigger dollar inflow than foreign direct investment.
Mexico sits on the last investment-grade rung at Moody's, held down chiefly by the cost of supporting Pemex, the state oil company.
